summary: Gruber reflects on WWDC 2025 one week later, noting Apple learned from its 2024 mistakes by only announcing features ready to ship this fall rather than making unfulfilled promises. He discusses the switch to year-based OS version numbers, praising the logic while noting it codifies Apple's annual release cadence as official policy. He celebrates iPadOS finally getting simple windowing after the overly complex Stage Manager detour. The bulk of the piece examines Liquid Glass, Apple's new design language, contrasting Alan Dye's 'soporific' introduction unfavorably with Steve Jobs's thrilling Aqua unveiling in 2000, while acknowledging the design itself is exciting if rough around the edges.
key_points: Apple corrected course from 2024 by only announcing features that are already in developer betas and planned to ship this fall, restoring credibility
Year-based version numbers quietly codify what was already true: Apple commits to annual major OS releases across all platforms, a hallmark of the Tim Cook era
iPadOS windowing finally delivers the simple, obvious approach that should have preceded Stage Manager, which Apple confusingly shipped first three years ago
Liquid Glass draws direct parallels to Aqua from 2000 — both exciting and fresh on debut, both initially over-the-top and half-baked in places, both expected to be refined over time
The internal development of Liquid Glass was chaotic, with builds described as a 'complete mess' just weeks before WWDC, though it came together for the developer betas
money_quotes: It takes confidence to promise only what you know you can ship, and it takes execution to ship what you've promised.
This rigorous annual schedule is a hallmark of the Tim Cook era at Apple, and clearly reflects his personality (as the erratic/idiosyncratic schedule of the mid-2000s reflected Steve Jobs's).
Apple, three years ago, jumped straight to a more complex, more fiddly option — Stage Manager — and only now has added the simpler, more obvious, not fiddly at all option (windowing).
I think iPadOS has finally arrived at a place where showing more than one app or document at a time on-screen is what it should have been all along: easy and obvious.
Re-watching Jobs's introduction of Aqua for the umpteenth time, I still find it enthralling. I found Alan Dye's introduction of Liquid Glass to be soporific, if not downright horseshitty.
It's exciting, it's fresh, it fundamentally looks and feels very cool in general — and but in practice quite a few aspects of it feel a bit over-the-top and/or half-baked.
As for why, it should be enough to justify Liquid Glass simply for the sake of looking cool.
key_insight: WWDC 2025 represents Apple returning to form — shipping what it promises, simplifying what it overcomplicated, and launching an ambitious new design language that, like Aqua before it, will need time to mature.
